items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What are the three primary colours in paint (subtractive)?
items.correct: Red, yellow, and blue (or magenta, yellow, cyan in print).
items.why: Students confuse light (additive: RGB) with paint/print (subtractive: RYB or CMY). RGB mixes light to white; RYB/CMY mixes pigment toward black - different colour models, different primaries.

items.correct: Visual equilibrium achieved with different elements of equal visual weight on each side.
items.why: Students confuse asymmetry with imbalance. Asymmetrical balance is still BALANCE - achieved through unequal but visually weighted elements (e.g., a small dark shape balancing a large light one).
Correct answer: f-stops. “ISO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Cartier-Bresson. “Ansel Adams” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 1842. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
